info.topictitle: B3.2.13 Drainage of excess tissue fluid into lymph ducts (HL)

markdown:
## Drainage of Excess Tissue Fluid into Lymph Ducts


The **lymphatic system plays a crucial role in draining excess tissue fluid**, also known as **interstitial fluid**, from tissues and returning it to blood circulation.

<definition term="Lymphatic system">A network of vessels, tissues, and organs that work together to drain excess tissue fluid and return it to the blood, while also aiding immune function.</definition>

![Lymphatic system](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/1d5de1c829fc375cb3db7f3cfd202ae61e53920c-800x1082.jpg)

## Tissue Fluid and the Lymphatic System


1. **Capillaries leak plasma**, forming **tissue fluid**, which delivers oxygen and nutrients to cells.
2. **Most tissue fluid is reabsorbed**, but **about 3 liters per day** remains in tissues.
3. **Without removal**, this excess fluid would **cause swelling (oedema)**.
4. The **lymphatic system** drains excess fluid via **lymph ducts**, returning it to the blood.

![Tissue fluid formation and drainage](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/977103186a4779e1a0ca7a08cd659792faf0cfab-1760x1000.png)

## Lymphatic Vessels: Structure for Efficient Drainage


### Thin Walls & Gaps for Easy Entry


1. **Lymphatic vessels** have **thin, permeable walls** made of endothelial cells.
2. **Gaps between cells** act as **one-way flaps**, allowing fluid in but not out.
3. **Blind-ended structure** ensures fluid moves **into** the vessel and doesn’t escape.

![Structure of lymph ducts](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/094dbbae5fec247afa6c7bbb7c6f81a4b6d4af8f-960x1016.jpg)

<callout type="tip">The permeability of lymphatic vessels, due to their thin walls and gaps, makes them **highly effective at collecting excess tissue fluid and preventing swelling.**</callout>

### Valves: Keeping Lymph Moving in One Direction


<definition term="Lymph">Lymph is a **clear, watery fluid **that **moves through the lymphatic system** and **carries nutrients, white blood cells, and other substances **throughout the body.</definition>

1. **Low pressure** in lymphatic vessels means fluid could flow backward.
2. **Valves prevent backflow, **they **open in one direction** and close if fluid moves the wrong way.
3. **Skeletal muscles assist, **as they contract, they **push lymph forward**, just like veins.

![Valves](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/96bfde3374ef9c911779086b5ff68bd89f06ca00-2560x1019.jpg)

<callout type="analogy">* Picture squeezing a tube of toothpaste. 
* The pressure you apply pushes the toothpaste forward, but it doesn’t flow back because of the tube’s design. 
* Similarly, valves in lymphatic vessels prevent backflow, keeping lymph moving toward the heart.</callout>

<callout type="warning">* Don’t confuse lymphatic valves with heart valves.
* While both prevent backflow, lymphatic valves are smaller and distributed throughout the lymphatic vessels, not confined to specific junctions.</callout>

### Returning Lymph to Blood Circulation


1. Lymph **must rejoin the bloodstream**. 
2. This happens via **two major lymphatic ducts**:
  1. **Thoracic Duct** – **Largest lymph vessel**, drains **most of the body** into the **left subclavian vein**.
  2. **Right Lymphatic Duct** – Drains lymph from **right head, neck, chest, and arm** into the **right subclavian vein**.
3. Once in the **subclavian veins**, lymph **mixes with blood** and re-enters circulation.

<callout type="note">Returning lymph to the bloodstream not only maintains fluid balance but also ensures the circulation of immune cells and filtered pathogens throughout the body.</callout>

## Why Is This Important?


1. **Prevents Oedema** – Removes excess fluid to avoid swelling.
2. **Boosts Immune Defense** – Transports **immune cells** to **lymph nodes** to filter pathogens.
3. **Helps Nutrient Transport** – **Absorbs fats** from digestion via **lacteals** in the small intestine.

<callout type="self_review">* What is the role of valves in the lymphatic system?
* How do the thin walls of lymph ducts facilitate their function?</callout>
